title_en: Divine Existence, Attributes, and the Maturidi Positions
title_id: Keberadaan Ilahi, Sifat-Sifat, dan Posisi-Posisi Maturidi
content_id:
Inti dari konten teologis Sharh al-Maqasid adalah pembahasan tentang keberadaan, esensi, dan sifat-sifat ilahi - pertanyaan-pertanyaan yang berada di pusat semua perdebatan kalam Islam klasik. Penyajian at-Taftazani sangat berharga karena ia terlibat secara sistematis dengan tradisi Asy'ari dan Maturidi dan mencatat persetujuan dan perbedaan mereka.
Mengenai bukti keberadaan Allah, at-Taftazani menyajikan dan membela berbagai argumen. Yang paling mendasar dalam tradisi kalam adalah argumen dari kontingensitas (dalil al-imkan): dunia adalah kontingen, hal-hal kontingen memerlukan sebab bagi keberadaannya, dan rantai sebab tidak dapat mundur tanpa batas, sehingga harus ada Wujud Perlu yang merupakan sebab utama dari semua keberadaan kontingen. Ia juga membahas argumen kosmologis dari kemunculan temporal (dalil al-huduts): dunia muncul setelah tidak ada, dan apa pun yang muncul memerlukan sebab, sehingga dunia memiliki sebab yang keberadaannya perlu dan abadi.
Mengenai sifat-sifat ilahi, penyajian at-Taftazani mencerminkan pendekatan mazhab Maturidi yang agak berbeda terhadap sifat kehendak ilahi. Mazhab Asy'ari berpendapat bahwa Allah menghendaki segala sesuatu, termasuk tindakan manusia yang tidak taat - bahwa tidak ada yang terjadi tanpa kehendak Allah, dipahami sebagai sifat penentuan (takhsis) yang memilih di antara kemungkinan-kemungkinan. Mazhab Maturidi menerima bahwa Allah menghendaki segala sesuatu tetapi berpendapat bahwa Allah tidak menghendaki tindakan ketidaktaatan manusia dalam arti yang sama dengan Ia menghendaki tindakan ketaatan - bahwa ada perbedaan antara apa yang Allah kehendaki secara universal sebagai penguasa dan apa yang Ia kehendaki sebagai persoalan persetujuan dan perintah.
Perbedaan ini berkaitan dengan perbedaan lain: mazhab Maturidi berpendapat bahwa Allah menciptakan tindakan manusia tetapi bahwa manusia memiliki kemampuan nyata (kasb) dalam arti yang lebih kuat dari yang diatributkan oleh mazhab Asy'ari. Kedua mazhab sedang mencoba bernavigasi antara determinisme manusia penuh (jabr), yang akan membuat tanggung jawab moral tidak koheren, dan kemampuan bebas manusia penuh (qadr), yang akan mengorbankan kedaulatan ilahi. Solusi yang mereka kembangkan berkaitan erat tetapi berbeda secara halus.
At-Taftazani juga membahas sifat kalam ilahi dan hubungannya dengan Al-Quran, sifat pengetahuan dan hubungannya dengan pengetahuan dan tindakan manusia, serta berbagai kategori nama dan sifat ilahi yang muncul dalam Al-Quran dan hadis. Penanganannya di seluruh teks bertujuan pada komprehensivitas: ia ingin mencakup bukan hanya posisi-posisi tetapi argumen-argumen untuk dan terhadap masing-masing, respons yang tersedia untuk berbagai mazhab, dan struktur logis keseluruhan perdebatan.
content_en:
The heart of Sharh al-Maqasid's theological content is its treatment of the divine existence, essence, and attributes — the questions that were at the center of all classical Islamic kalam debate. At-Taftazani's presentation is particularly valuable because he engages systematically with both the Ash'ari and Maturidi traditions and notes their agreements and differences.

On the proof for God's existence, at-Taftazani presents and defends multiple arguments. The most fundamental in the kalam tradition is the argument from contingency (dalil al-imkan): the world is contingent, contingent things require a cause for their existence, and the chain of causes cannot regress infinitely, so there must be a Necessary Being that is the ultimate cause of all contingent existence. He also discusses the cosmological argument from temporal origination (dalil al-huduth): the world came into existence after not existing, and whatever comes into existence requires a cause, so the world has a cause whose existence is necessary and eternal.

On the divine attributes, at-Taftazani's presentation reflects the Maturidi tradition's somewhat different approach to the attribute of divine will. The Ash'ari school held that God wills all things, including human acts of disobedience — that nothing occurs without God's will, understood as the attribute of specification (takhsis) that selects among possibilities. The Maturidi school accepted that God wills all things but held that God does not will human acts of disobedience in the same sense that He wills acts of obedience — that there is a distinction between what God wills universally as sovereign and what He wills as a matter of approval and command.

This distinction relates to another difference: the Maturidi school held that God created human acts but that humans have real agency (kasb) in a stronger sense than the Ash'ari school attributed to it. Both schools were trying to navigate between full human determinism (jabr), which would make moral responsibility incoherent, and full human independent agency (qadr), which would compromise divine sovereignty. The solutions they developed are closely related but subtly different.

At-Taftazani also addresses the attributes of divine speech and its relationship to the Quran, the attribute of knowledge and its relation to human knowledge and action, and the various categories of divine names and attributes that appear in the Quran and hadith. His treatment throughout aims at comprehensiveness: he wants to cover not just the positions but the arguments for and against each, the responses available to different schools, and the overall logical structure of the debates.
content_ar: The القلب of Sharh al-Maqasid's theological content is its treatment of the divine existence, essence, and attributes — the questions that were at the center of all الكلاسيكي Islamic علم الكلام debate. At-Taftazani's presentation is particularly valuable لأن he engages systematically with both the الأشعري and الماتريدي traditions and notes their agreements and differences. On the proof for God's existence, at-Taftazani presents and defends multiple arguments. The most الأساسي in the علم الكلام التراث is the argument from contingency (dalil al-imkan): the الدنيا is contingent, contingent things require a cause for their existence, and the chain of causes cannot regress infinitely, لذا there must be a Necessary Being that is the ultimate cause of all contingent existence. He أيضاً discusses the cosmological argument from temporal origination (dalil al-huduth): the الدنيا came into existence بعد not existing, and whatever comes into existence requires a cause, لذا the الدنيا has a cause whose existence is necessary and eternal. On the divine attributes, at-Taftazani's presentation reflects the الماتريدي التراث's somewhat different approach to the attribute of divine will. The الأشعري school held that God wills all things, بما في ذلك human acts of disobedience — that nothing occurs without God's will, understood as the attribute of specification (takhsis) that selects among possibilities. The الماتريدي school accepted that God wills all things but held that God does not will human acts of disobedience in the same sense that He wills acts of obedience — that there is a distinction between what God wills universally as sovereign and what He wills as a matter of approval and command. This distinction relates to another difference: the الماتريدي school held that God created human acts but that humans have real agency (kasb) in a stronger sense than the الأشعري school attributed to it. Both schools were trying to navigate between full human determinism (jabr), which would make moral المسؤولية incoherent, and full human independent agency (qadr), which would compromise divine sovereignty. The solutions they developed are closely related but subtly different. At-Taftazani أيضاً addresses the attributes of divine speech and its relationship to the القرآن الكريم, the attribute of العلم and its relation to human العلم and action, and the various categories of divine names and attributes that appear in the القرآن الكريم and الحديث. His treatment throughout aims at comprehensiveness: he wants to cover not just the positions but the arguments for and against each, the responses available to different schools, and the overall logical structure of the debates.
